title: Charmed Life - Chapter 13 (Fragment 1)
text:
Chapter Ten

10

C
 AT WATCHED his moment and, when Mr. Saunders called them to lessons, he caught Roger‚Äôs arm and whispered, ‚ÄúLook, Gwendolen‚Äôs turned Euphemia into a frog and‚Äî‚Äù

Roger gave a great snore of laughter. Cat had to wait for him to stop.

‚ÄúAnd she won‚Äôt turn her back. Can you ?‚Äù

Roger tried to look serious, but laughter kept breaking through. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know. Probably not, unless she‚Äôll tell you what spell she used. Finding out which spell without knowing is Advanced Magic, and I‚Äôm not on that yet. Oh, how funny!‚Äù He bent over the table and yelled with laughter.

Naturally, Mr. Saunders appeared at the door, remarking that the time for telling jokes was after lessons. They had to go through to the schoolroom. Naturally, Cat found Janet had sat in his desk by mistake. He got her out as quietly as he could and sat in it himself, distractedly wondering how he could find out which spell Gwendolen had used.

It was the most uncomfortable morning Cat had ever known. He had forgotten to tell Janet that the only thing Gwendolen knew about was witchcraft. Janet, as he had rather suspected, knew a lot, about a lot of things. But it all applied to her own world. About the only subject she would have been safe in was simple arithmetic. And Mr. Saunders chose that morning to give her a History test. Cat, as he scratched away left-handed at an English essay, could see the panic growing on Janet‚Äôs face.

‚ÄúWhat do you mean, Henry the Fifth?‚Äù barked Mr. Saunders. ‚ÄúRichard the Second was on the throne until long after Agincourt. What was his greatest magical achievement?‚Äù

‚ÄúDefeating the French,‚Äù Janet guessed. Mr. Saunders looked so exasperated that she babbled, ‚ÄúWell, I think it was. He hampered the French with iron underwear, and the English wore wool, so they didn‚Äôt stick in the mud, and probably their longbows were enchanted too. That would account for them not missing.‚Äù

‚ÄúWho,‚Äù said Mr. Saunders, ‚Äúdo you imagine won the Battle of Agincourt?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe English,‚Äù said Janet. This of course was true for her world, but the panic-stricken look on her face as she said it suggested that she suspected the opposite was true in this world. Which, of course, it was.

Mr. Saunders put his hands to his head. ‚ÄúNo, no, no! The French ! Don‚Äôt you know anything , girl?‚Äù

Janet looked to be near tears. Cat was terrified. She was going to break down any second and tell Mr. Saunders she was not Gwendolen. She did not have Cat‚Äôs reasons for keeping quiet. ‚ÄúGwendolen never knows anything,‚Äù he remarked loudly, hoping Janet would take the hint. She did. She sighed with relief and relaxed.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm aware of that,‚Äù said Mr. Saunders. ‚ÄúBut somewhere, somewhere inside that marble head there must be a little cell of gray matter. So I keep looking.‚Äù

Unfortunately, Janet, in her relief, became almost jolly. ‚ÄúWould you like to take my head apart and look?‚Äù she asked.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt tempt me!‚Äù cried Mr. Saunders. He hid his eyes with one knobby hand and fended at Janet with the other. He looked so funny that Janet laughed. This was so unlike Gwendolen that Mr. Saunders lowered his hand across his nose and stared at her suspiciously over it. ‚ÄúWhat have you been up to now?‚Äù

‚ÄúNothing,‚Äù Janet said guiltily.

‚ÄúHm,‚Äù said Mr. Saunders, in a way which made both Cat and Janet very uncomfortable.

vocabulary.definition: to explain or be the reason for something
vocabulary.example: That would account for them not missing

vocabulary.definition: a higher or more difficult level of magical practice
vocabulary.example: Finding out which spell without knowing is Advanced Magic

vocabulary.definition: the branch of mathematics dealing with numbers and their operations
vocabulary.example: she would have been safe in was simple arithmetic

vocabulary.definition: words that often go together
vocabulary.example: meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations

vocabulary.definition: giving details about
vocabulary.example: descriptive adjectives

vocabulary.definition: in a way showing someone‚Äôs attention is not focused
vocabulary.example: sat in it himself, distractedly wondering

vocabulary.definition: placed under a magical spell
vocabulary.example: their longbows were enchanted too

vocabulary.definition: very annoyed or frustrated
vocabulary.example: Mr. Saunders looked so exasperated that she babbled

vocabulary.definition: to give an answer or opinion without sure knowledge
vocabulary.example: Janet guessed

vocabulary.definition: to make something difficult to do or progress slower
vocabulary.example: He hampered the French with iron underwear

vocabulary.definition: a slight or indirect indication or suggestion
vocabulary.example: hoping Janet would take the hint

vocabulary.definition: happy and cheerful
vocabulary.example: Janet, in her relief, became almost jolly

vocabulary.definition: a group that judges or makes a decision
vocabulary.example: Janet, in her relief, became almost jolly

vocabulary.definition: having lumps or knobs
vocabulary.example: hid his eyes with one knobby hand

vocabulary.definition: sudden uncontrollable fear or anxiety
vocabulary.example: could see the panic growing on Janet‚Äôs face

vocabulary.definition: overcome with sudden, uncontrollable fear or anxiety
vocabulary.example: the panic-stricken look on her face

vocabulary.definition: to say something, especially as a comment or observation
vocabulary.example: he remarked loudly, hoping Janet would take the hint

vocabulary.definition: not in danger
vocabulary.example: About the only subject she would have been safe in was simple arithmetic

vocabulary.definition: to breathe out slowly and audibly, expressing tiredness, sadness, or relief
vocabulary.example: She sighed with relief and relaxed

vocabulary.definition: a magic word or formula believed to have magical power
vocabulary.example: Finding out which spell without knowing is Advanced Magic

vocabulary.definition: seriously affected by an unpleasant feeling or disease
vocabulary.example: panic-stricken look on her face

vocabulary.definition: to believe something is likely to be true, often without certain proof
vocabulary.example: as he had rather suspected, knew a lot

vocabulary.definition: feelings or beliefs that someone is guilty or something is wrong
vocabulary.example: stared at her suspiciously

vocabulary.definition: a drop of clear salty liquid from the eyes
vocabulary.example: Janet looked to be near tears

vocabulary.definition: to speak very quietly, using the breath but not the voice
vocabulary.example: he caught Roger‚Äôs arm and whispered, ‚ÄúLook, Gwendolen‚Äôs turned Euphemia into a frog and‚Äî‚Äù
